is a cultural [taboo](/wiki/Taboo "Taboo") against speaking or writing the given names of exalted persons, notably in [China](/wiki/China "China") and within the [Chinese cultural sphere](/wiki/Chinese_cultural_sphere "Chinese cultural sphere"). It was enforced by several laws throughout Imperial China, but its cultural and possibly religious origins predate the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty"). Not respecting the appropriate naming taboos was considered a sign of lacking education and respect, and brought shame both to the offender and the offended person.

A **naming taboo** is a cultural [taboo](/wiki/Taboo "Taboo") against speaking or writing the given names of exalted persons, notably in [China](/wiki/China "China") and within the [Chinese cultural sphere](/wiki/Chinese_cultural_sphere "Chinese cultural sphere"). It was enforced by several laws throughout Imperial China, but its cultural and possibly religious origins predate the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty"). Not respecting the appropriate naming taboos was considered a sign of lacking education and respect, and brought shame both to the offender and the offended person.

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Naming_taboo&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Types")]

* The *naming taboo of the state* (國諱 *guóhuì*) discouraged the use of the emperor's given name and those of his ancestors. For example, during the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty"), [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang")'s given name 政 Zhèng (< [\*](/wiki/Reconstruction_of_Old_Chinese "Reconstruction of Old Chinese")‍OC [B-S](/wiki/Reconstructions_of_Old_Chinese#Baxter–Sagart_(2014) "Reconstructions of Old Chinese"): \**teŋ-s*) was avoided, and the first month of the year, the *upright month* (正月; *Zhèngyuè*) had its pronunciation modified to *Zhēngyuè* (OC B-S: \**teŋ*, like 征 "to go on a long journey, to go on a military campaign")[[1]](#cite_note-1) and then further renamed as the *proper/upright month* (端月; *Duānyuè* < OC, B-S \**tˤor*]). The strength of this taboo was reinforced by law; transgressors could expect serious punishment for writing an emperor's name without modifications. In 1777, [Wang Xihou](/wiki/Wang_Xihou "Wang Xihou"), in his dictionary, criticized the [Kangxi dictionary](/wiki/Kangxi_dictionary "Kangxi dictionary") and wrote the [Qianlong Emperor](/wiki/Qianlong_Emperor "Qianlong Emperor")'s name without leaving out any stroke as required. This disrespect resulted in his and his [family's executions](/wiki/Nine_exterminations "Nine exterminations") and confiscation of their property (though all Wang Xihou's relatives were pardoned and spared execution).[[2]](#cite_note-2) This type of naming taboo is no longer observed in modern China.
* The *naming taboo of the clan* (家諱) discouraged the use of the names of one's own ancestors. Generally, ancestor names going back to seven generations were avoided. In diplomatic documents and letters between clans, each clan's naming taboos were observed.
* The *naming taboo of the holinesses* (聖人諱) discouraged the use of the names of respected people. For example, writing the name of [Confucius](/wiki/Confucius "Confucius") was taboo during the [Jin dynasty](/wiki/Jin_dynasty_(1115%E2%80%931234) "Jin dynasty (1115–1234)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Methods to avoid offense

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Naming_taboo&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Methods to avoid offense")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Omitting_a_stroke.png/250px-Omitting_a_stroke.png)](/wiki/File:Omitting_a_stroke.png)

Avoidance of naming taboo: Example of omitting a stroke. The last stroke of each character of the Kangxi Emperor's given name "玄" (xuán) and "燁" (yè) is omitted. Failure to do this led to execution, like that of [Wang Xihou](/wiki/Wang_Xihou "Wang Xihou").

There were three ways to avoid using a taboo character:

* Changing the character to another, usually a [synonym](/wiki/Synonym "Synonym") or one which sounded similar to the character being avoided. For example, the [Black Warrior](/wiki/Black_Tortoise "Black Tortoise") Gate (玄武門; Xuanwu Gate) of the [Forbidden City](/wiki/Forbidden_City "Forbidden City") was renamed the [Gate of Divine Might](/wiki/Gate_of_Divine_Might "Gate of Divine Might") (神武門; Shenwu Gate) in order to avoid using a character from the [Kangxi Emperor](/wiki/Kangxi_Emperor "Kangxi Emperor")'s name, Xuanye (玄燁).
* Leaving the character as a blank.
* Omitting a stroke in the character, usually the final stroke.

## In history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Naming_taboo&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: In history")]

Throughout Chinese history, there were emperors whose names contained common characters who would try to alleviate the burden of the populace in practicing name avoidance. For example, [Emperor Xuan of Han](/wiki/Emperor_Xuan_of_Han "Emperor Xuan of Han"), whose given name Bingyi (病已) contained two very common characters, changed his name to Xun (詢), a far less common character, with the stated purpose of making it easier for his people to avoid using his name.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Similarly, [Emperor Taizong of Tang](/wiki/Emperor_Taizong_of_Tang "Emperor Taizong of Tang"), whose given name Shimin (世民) also contained two very common characters, ordered that name avoidance only required the avoidance of the characters *Shi* and *Min* in direct succession and that it did not require the avoidance of those characters in isolation.

However, Emperor Taizong's son [Emperor Gaozong of Tang](/wiki/Emperor_Gaozong_of_Tang "Emperor Gaozong of Tang") effectively made this edict ineffective after his death, by requiring the complete avoidance of the characters *Shi* and *Min*, necessitating the chancellor [Li Shiji](/wiki/Li_Shiji "Li Shiji") to change his name to Li Ji.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In later dynasties, princes were frequently given names that contained uncommon characters to make it easier for the public to avoid them, should they become emperor later in life.

During the rule of the [Ming Emperor of Han](/wiki/Ming_Emperor_of_Han "Ming Emperor of Han") (Liu Zhuang), whose personal name was Zhuang, most people with surname [Zhuang](/wiki/Zhuang_(surname) "Zhuang (surname)") (莊) were ordered to change their names to its synonym [Yan](/wiki/Yan_(surname)#.E5.9A.B4 "Yan (surname)") (嚴).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The custom of naming taboo had a built-in contradiction: without knowing what the emperors' names were, one could hardly be expected to avoid them, thus somehow the emperors' names had to be informally transmitted to the populace to allow them to take cognizance of and thus avoid using said characters. In one famous incident in 435, during the [Northern Wei](/wiki/Northern_Wei "Northern Wei") dynasty, [Goguryeo](/wiki/Goguryeo "Goguryeo") ambassadors made a formal request that the imperial government issue them a document containing the emperors' names so that they could avoid offending the emperor while submitting their king's petition. [Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei](/wiki/Emperor_Taiwu_of_Northern_Wei "Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei") agreed and issued them such a document.[[6]](#cite_note-6) However, the mechanism of how the regular populace would be able to learn the emperors' names remained generally unclear throughout Chinese history.

This taboo is important to keep in mind when studying ancient historical texts from the cultural sphere, as historical characters and/or locations may be renamed if they happen to share a name with the emperor in power (or previous emperors of the same dynasty) when the text was written. Thus, the study of naming taboos can also help date an ancient text.

## In other countries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Naming_taboo&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: In other countries")]

### Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Naming_taboo&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Japan")]

Japan was also influenced by the naming taboo. In modern Japan, it concerns only the [successive emperors](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan"). For example, whether oral or written, people only refer to the reigning emperor as Tennō Heika (天皇陛下; his Majesty the Emperor) or [Kinjō Heika](/wiki/Reigning_Emperor "Reigning Emperor") (今上陛下; his current Majesty).
See also [posthumous name](/wiki/Posthumous_name#Japan "Posthumous name").
  
Historically, it was considered very rude among upper class to call someone else's real name, even if it was the lord calling his vassals. Calling someone else's real name was equivalent to picking a fight. Titles or pseudonyms were often used when calling others in place of their real names.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Vietnam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Naming_taboo&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Vietnam")]

| Chinese Character | Original Reading | Deviated Reading | Name |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 承 | thằng | thừa | [Trần Thằng](/wiki/Tr%E1%BA%A7n_Th%E1%BB%ABa "Trần Thừa") (陳承) |
| 利 | lị | lợi | [Lê Lị](/wiki/L%C3%AA_L%E1%BB%A3i "Lê Lợi") (黎利) |
| 黄 | hoàng | huỳnh | [Nguyễn Hoàng](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Ho%C3%A0ng "Nguyễn Hoàng") (阮潢) |
| 金/今 | câm | kim | [Nguyễn Cam](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Kim "Nguyễn Kim") (阮淦) |
| 周 | chu | châu | [Nguyễn Phúc Chu](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Ph%C3%BAc_Chu "Nguyễn Phúc Chu") (阮福淍) |
| 映 | ánh | yếng | [Nguyễn Phúc Ánh](/wiki/Gia_Long "Gia Long") (阮福暎) |
| 時 | thì | thời | [Nguyễn Phúc Thì](/wiki/T%E1%BB%B1_%C4%90%E1%BB%A9c "Tự Đức") (阮福時) |
| 山 | san | sơn | [Nguyễn Phúc Vĩnh San](/wiki/Duy_T%C3%A2n "Duy Tân") (阮福永珊) |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Nguyendynastynamingtaboos.png/250px-Nguyendynastynamingtaboos.png)](/wiki/File:Nguyendynastynamingtaboos.png)

Naming taboo during the [Nguyễn dynasty](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_dynasty "Nguyễn dynasty")

In Vietnam, naming taboos were also observed since the beginning of Vietnamese independence. Characters in texts avoid taboos by omitting strokes (such as in the case with [Hồ Thị Hoa](/wiki/H%E1%BB%93_Th%E1%BB%8B_Hoa "Hồ Thị Hoa") 胡氏華 where the last stroke of 華 was not written), using variant characters (such as with Lê Lợi 黎利, 利 was written as [⿱⿰巜巜⿰刂禾]), and using similar characters (such as in the case with Tự Đức, the character 辰 thần/thìn was used to write 時 thì; it also has the meaning of "time").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Similar to Southern China, the taboos also applied to pronunciation as well. One such example is the name of rice paper, [bánh đa](/wiki/B%C3%A1nh_tr%C3%A1ng "Bánh tráng") (Northern dialect), originally named bánh tráng, it was renamed due to it being homophonous with [Trịnh Tráng](/wiki/Tr%E1%BB%8Bnh_Tr%C3%A1ng "Trịnh Tráng") 鄭梉. This is also the reason why some chữ Hán have multiple readings due to naming taboos. The character 武 has two readings unlike in Mandarin where it only has one. The character 武 is a surname pronounced as [Vũ](/wiki/V%C5%A9 "Vũ") in the north, but is known as Võ in the South due to the naming taboo of Nguyễn Phúc Miên Vũ (阮福綿宇).[[8]](#cite_note-ThierryInscriptions-8)
